Barrio Viejo. Medianoche. La lluvia no d: Old District. Midnight. The rain hasn't relented since the bells rang in full blast.
Así que aquí es. Una mujer se esfuma en: So here it is. A woman vanishes in the middle of mass and nobody sees a thing. Curious.
Marta Soler. Treinta y dos años. Entró e: Marta Soler. Thirty-two. She walked into the church... and never came out.
El barrio duerme a medias tras las contr: The neighborhood sleeps in half-light behind the shutters. Someone saw something. Somebody always sees something.
Marta Soler lived two streets from here. I'll start at her house.
En la agenda de Marta, una cita sin nomb: In Marta's agenda, an unnamed appointment on the night she vanished.
El portal cede a la primera. Ni forzado: The front door gives at first. Not forced nor locked: ajar as if Marta had gone out for bread and could return any moment. She won't come back.
Primera regla: la casa de una persona ha: Rule one: a house speaks louder than its owner. Let's see what yours whispers, Marta.
Un piso pequeño, ordenado con esmero de: A small apartment, kept tidy with the care of someone who has little and guards it. A half-drunk cup of tea on the table, with a thin cold film on its surface. The bed is bare. Everything hangs in a suspended beat.
El té sin terminar. La cama deshecha. Sa: The unfinished tea. The disheveled bed. He left in a hurry, at night, without thinking of taking another cup— or without the means to do so.
En la estantería, una foto: Marta y otra: On the shelf, a photo: Marta and another very similar woman, embracing, laughing on a gray beach. Behind it, in pen, it reads: Sisters, come what may
Una hermana. Alguien a quien esto le va: A sister. Someone who is going to get split in two. Taking note: we must find her.
Sobre la mesa, la agenda abierta por la: On the table, the agenda open to yesterday’s date. Most of the days are blank. Only the night slot has something written, in nervous handwriting.
'23:00'. Una hora. Sin nombre. ¿A quién: 23:00. One hour. No name. Who do you schedule at eleven at night and prefer not to write their name even in their own agenda?
A alguien a quien se teme. O a alguien p: Someone you fear. Or someone forbidden. Sometimes they're the same person.
En el cajón de la mesilla, bajo un rosar: In the drawer of the desk, beneath a rosary, a bundle of religious prints of St. Jude Thaddeus. The one about lost causes. And one, wrinkled, with a pencil‑sketched phone number, almost erased.
Rezaba a las causas perdidas y guardaba: He prayed to the lost causes and kept a number without a name. Marta, you were drowning and didn't let anyone know.
La ventana da justo a la torre de San Jo: The window looks straight onto the Tower of San José, black against an electric sky. From the sill, someone has been staring at it for a long time: a row of cups and a chair turned toward the glass.
Se sentaba a mirar el campanario. Como s: He sat to stare at the bell tower, as if whatever he feared— or what he expected—was coming from there.
Clavada en la pared de la cocina, una ci: Pinned to the kitchen wall, a parish bulletin reads 'Mass at 23:00. We ask for punctuality.' Same hour as the appointment.
La cita y la misa a la misma hora. No fu: The appointment and the mass at the same hour. He wasn't there to pray: he was meeting someone under a blanket of the whole neighborhood. What better alibi than being among a hundred witnesses.
Necesito a alguien que estuviera dentro: I need someone who was inside that mass and had their ears tight. Don Emilio, the neighbor, never misses one. I'll start with him.
Nano, el ex violento de Marta, PARECÍA e: Nano, Marta's violent ex, SEEMED the culprit... but he spent the night in the dungeon.
Un vecino que rondaba a Marta; lloraba d: A neighbor who stalked Marta; he wept out of sorrow, not remorse.
Marta debía dinero; el prestamista la qu: Marta owed money; the lender wanted her alive and paying, not vanished.
Un sintecho que dormía en la iglesia; ni: A homeless who slept in the church; he didn’t even notice the commotion.
La maldición del campanario: The curse of the bell tower
El rumor de que la torre 'se traga' gent: The rumor that the tower "swallows" people. Neighborhood superstition.
El bar del Nano huele a lejía y a cervez: The Nano's bar smells like bleach and stale beer. Behind the counter, a burly guy with chiseled knuckles looks at you like someone would look at the police: with disgust.
Nano. Fuiste el novio de Marta Soler. El: Nano. You were Marta Soler's boyfriend, the one who left her with two broken ribs last winter.
Eso es agua pasada. Y si ha desaparecido: That’s water over the top. If it’s vanished, I had nothing to do with it. You all have a grudge against me in this neighborhood.
Un hombre que pega a su pareja y luego e: A man who hits his partner and then she slips away. You'll see it starts with you.
Empieza por donde quieras, pero mira ant: Start wherever you want, but check your own file first. The night of the mass I was in YOUR dungeon, because of a fight on the 12. Twenty-four hours. Signed and sealed.
Una llamada rápida a comisaría lo confir: A quick call to the precinct confirms it: Nano spent the entire night of the disappearance behind bars. Impossible he was in the church.
(Coartada de hierro. El sospechoso obvio: (Iron alibi. The obvious suspect is never the good one. Dump Nano and keep going.)
Perdona la visita, Nano. Aunque seas un: Sorry to intrude, Nano. Even if you're a rough rascal, tonight you're not my rough rascal.
Durante las campanas se oyó un grito jun: During the bells a scream rang near the altar.
El portal huele a humedad y a geranios m: The portal reeks of damp and dead geraniums. You call twice before a chain squeaks and a sliver of yellow light opens in the gloom.
¿Quién anda...? Ah. Usted es la detectiv: Who's there? Ah. You're the city detective. I was expecting you, though you might not believe it.
Don Emilio quita la cadena. Es un hombre: Don Emilio removes the chain. He's a small, hunched man in a wool sweater that ends up too big for him. His hands tremble, but not from fear: it's from years.
Pase, pase, que se está mojando y aquí d: Come on in, you’re getting drenched, but at least there’s hot soup waiting inside. Take a seat. That chair’s the real deal; the other one’s a wobble.
Gracias. No le robaré mucho tiempo, Don: Thank you. I won't take up too much of your time, Don Emilio.
Tiempo es lo único que me sobra, hija. L: Time is the only thing I have left, kid. What I’m running out of is sleep. Since last night I haven’t been able to get any rest.
Sobre la cómoda, un retrato en blanco y: On the dresser, a black-and-white portrait of a young woman. It follows your gaze and smiles sadly.
Mi Amparo. Cuarenta años juntos y dos vi: My Amparo. Forty years together, and we two watched her leave little by little. That's why I go to Mass every night. I no longer pray for myself.
Anoche también estaba usted en la iglesi: You were also at the church last night.
En mi banco de siempre, el tercero por l: In my usual pew, the third from the left. From there you see the whole altar. And you hear. Oh, you really hear.
Cuénteme la noche. Desde el principio, s: Tell me the night. From the beginning, no rush.
Llovía como ahora, o peor. El padre Isma: It was raining like now, or worse. Father Ismael started late because people slipped in dripping. The church was full, a strange thing on a stormy night.
A media misa se soltó el viento y las ca: At mid‑mass the wind tore loose and the bells began to pound themselves in rhythm, like when I was a kid and they warned of a flood. A roar that swallowed even the sermon.
And then, between chimes and chimes… a scream. Just one. Brief, sharp. Near the altar.
Se le quiebra la voz. Aprieta el borde d: His voice cracks. He grips the edge of the table with white knuckles.
Me giré. La muchacha, la Marta, estaba e: I turned. The girl, Marta, was in the front row and suddenly she was gone, as if the floor had swallowed her. My blood went icy, detective. It went icy.
\"¿Reconoció la voz del grito?\": "Did you recognize the voice of the scream?"
Era de mujer, joven. Eso lo juraría. Per: It was a woman, kid. I would swear that. But with that clang… I couldn’t say whether she’d uttered a name or was just shouting to shout.
Aunque... hubo algo. Antes del grito me: Though... there was something. Before the scream I thought I heard quick footsteps from the side. Boots, not Sunday shoes. Boots.
\"¿Vio salir a alguien por la puerta?\": Did you see anyone leave through the door?
No, no... yo miraba al altar, como todos: No, no… I was staring at the altar like everyone else. Rosa watched the big door; she never leaves her spot. Ask her; she has a better view than I do.
\"¿Notó algo raro en Marta esos días?\": Did you notice anything strange with Marta those days?
La veía rezar mucho últimamente. Y mirar: I saw her praying a lot lately. And looking back toward the door, like someone waiting for someone who doesn't want to arrive.
Una noche la vi santiguarse tres veces s: One night I watched her sanctify herself three times in a row. Three. That's not what someone at peace would do.
¿Y cuando encendieron las luces? Después: And when did they turn on the lights? After the commotion.
El padre Ismael mandó callar y buscar. M: Father Ismael ordered us to shut up and search. We looked everywhere. Nothing. No girl, no clue. Just the empty bench and the fallen recliner.
La gente empezó a decir que había sido u: People started saying it had been a reverse miracle. I don’t believe in those things. Miracles don’t leave a recliner thrown by a kick.
Es usted mejor detective que la mitad de: You are a better detective than half of mine, Don Emilio.
Soy viejo, que no es lo mismo, pero se l: I'm old, not quite the same, but it looks like it. You learn to look at what's left in a place and what's missing.
Y esa noche, en el altar, faltaba una mu: And that night at the altar, a girl was missing and some booted steps were left over. Note it, kid. Note it well.
Un grito junto al altar, en plena tormen: A shout by the altar, in the middle of the storm. I note it. Thank you, Don Emilio.
No me las dé. Encuéntrela. Y si ya no se: Don't give me those. Find them. And if it can't be done… at least someone pays for the recliner kick. Go see Rosa. She was watching the door.
Nadie salió por la puerta principal: alg: No one walked out the main door - someone was watching.
El atrio de San José es un arco de piedr: San José's atrium is a charred stone arch where rain drums like buckshot. Beneath it, a bottle-green woman smokes with her back rigid, keeping her eyes on the big door.
La estaba viendo cruzar la plaza. Camina: I was watching her cross the square. You walk like someone on a hunt. You're the city girl, the detective.
Rosa, ¿verdad? Me han dicho que anoche e: Rosa, huh? Word's been that she was here last night, at the door.
Aquí estoy siempre. Vendo estampas y vel: I’ve been putting up shop here for forty years. I know this door better than my husband, may he rest in peace.
Da una calada larga. La brasa ilumina un: Take a long breath. The ember lights a hard face, with sharp cheekbones and eyes that don't blink.
Entonces, si alguien hubiera salido dura: So, if someone had left during Mass, you would have seen him.
No 'si'. Lo habría visto. Y no salió nad: No 'if'. I would’ve seen it. And nobody came out—not during the Mass or the commotion. Through that door neither Marta Soler nor the Holy Spirit emerged.
Es una afirmación muy rotunda para una n: That's a very solid statement for a stormy night.
Mire, hija. Cuando empezaron las campana: Look, kid. When the bells started pounding on their own, I got goosebumps and pressed myself against the rail like a slug. I didn’t even move to clasp my hands. If a fly had taken a stroll, I’d have counted it.
\"¿Y las otras puertas de la iglesia?\": What about the other doors of the church?
La lateral, la de la sacristía, estaba c: The side, the sacristy one, was locked. I even saw the priest toss it out at the start, knowing that there were plenty of ruthless folks coming in to steal the brush.
Y luego está la del campanario. Pero por: And then there's the one from the bell tower. But nobody goes in or out there during Mass. That key is kept by the sacristan, hanging from his belt.
(La del campanario. Guardemos eso.): The bell tower one. Let's keep that.
\"¿Se distrajo en algún momento?\": Did you get distracted at any point?
Not even a second, I've already told him. Well… I lie. There was one.
Cayó un rayo que iluminó la nave entera: A flash lit the whole ship like daylight, and all, all, we turned our heads to the glass. One instant. One blink.
(Un instante puede bastar. Todos mirando: An instant may be enough. Everyone looking up, and someone moving down.
No me mire así. Un rayo es un rayo. Cual: Don’t look at me like that. A ray is a ray. Everyone looks.
Me compraba una vela cada noche. Blanca,: I bought myself a candle every night. White, the cheapest. I always lit it in the same place: in front of San José, the impossible one.
Últimamente venía pálida, con ojeras. Y: Recently she had been pale, with dark circles. One night she asked me for a portrait of Saint Jude, the one of lost causes. She handed me something, but I don't know what to give it.
Rosa, si nadie salió por delante y las l: Rosa, if no one left from the front and the side doors were shut... how does a woman disappear from a packed church?
Eso pregúnteselo a Dios, o al diablo, qu: Ask that to God or the devil, because both are in this neighborhood. I only know what I saw. And what I didn’t see leave.
Si quiere ojos en la CALLE y no en la pu: If you want eyes on the STREET rather than at the door, talk to Tomás, the one at the corner store. From his counter he sees everything you think.
Si nadie salió por delante... salió por: If no one came out front… they went another way. And only one remains. Thanks, Rosa.
Marta discutió ayer con un hombre encapu: Marta argued with a hooded man yesterday.
El colmado de Tomás tiene la persiana a: Tomás's shop has the blinds half‑raised and a neon tube flickering over canned goods. It smells of stale coffee and damp sawdust. The clerk wipes the counter with a rag, even though it's already gleaming.
Cierro en diez minutos, deten... ah. Ust: I’ll close in ten minutes. Stop… ah. You’re not here to buy. It shows. You’re here for Marta.
It shows up a lot, apparently.
En este barrio, forastero que aparece de: In this part of town, outsider who shows up at night, he'll either be a new priest or trouble. And you don't look like a priest. Sit on the stool, it limps less than I do.
Deja el trapo. Tiene las manos grandes,: Leave the rag. He has big, cracked hands, from a lifetime of unloading boxes. His eyes, on the other hand, are quick and ready.
Marta venía cada noche. Leche, tabaco ru: Marta came every night. Milk, blond tobacco, and on Fridays a piece of nougat even if it wasn’t Christmas. She said the sugar helped her sleep. Good girl. One of those who paid and greeted.
¿Notó algo distinto en ella últimamente?: Did you notice anything off about her lately?
Estaba asustada. Y no de deber dinero, q: I was scared. Not of owing money—I know that fear. It was something else. I was watching the street before I went in. And once she told me to get out through the back door, the one in the warehouse.
Claro que la dejé. A una mujer con ese m: Sure I left her. You don’t slam the door on a woman who’s got fear in her eyes. I wish I’d asked who she was running from. I didn’t. That’s what I’m taking on now.
Golpea el mostrador, más con pena que co: He smacks the counter, more with pity than fury.
Y ayer lo vi. Aquí mismo, en la esquina,: And yesterday I saw him. Right there at the corner, under the flickering street lamp that was dead. He was arguing with a man. Tall. Hooded. I didn’t see his face; he made sure to keep it from me.
Ella le decía 'déjame en paz, ya te dije: She told him, leave me alone, I already told you no. He grabbed her by the arm, hard. I slipped out with a broom, mostly to make noise more than anything else, and when I reached the door she was gone. The rain swallowed him.
\"¿Le había visto antes por el barrio?\": Had you seen him before around town?
Nunca. Y mire que yo conozco hasta a los: Never. And look, I even know cats by name. That one wasn't from around here. It smelled of somewhere else, you get me? For money and for the city.
Los de aquí andan encogidos por la lluvi: The locals shrink under the rain. He walked straight, as if the rain were for others.
\"¿Recuerda algún detalle suyo?\": Do you recall any detail of his?
Botas. Buenas botas, de cuero, de las ca: Boots. Good leather boots, of the faces. And under the hood, a long coat of fine cloth. A little gentleman playing at being a shadow.
Ah, y un anillo. En la mano con que la a: Ah, and a ring. In the hand she held it with. Big, with a seal. It shone for a moment in the neon. Gold, or something that pretended to be it.
(Botas caras. Un anillo de sello. No es: Expensive boots. A seal ring. He's not a neighbour; he's the sort that rolls into the block.
\"¿Por qué no avisó a la comisaría?\": Why didn't he inform the police station?
¿A la comisaría? Ja. En este barrio, det: To the station? Ha. In this neighborhood, detective, the station is where complaints die. You learn to shut up and pull down the blinds.
Aunque el sargento Núñez... ese es disti: Even Sergeant Núñez… that’s a different matter. That one would care, if only they'd let him care.
Un hombre de fuera, con dinero, que la a: An outsider, with money, who pulls her by the arm the night before she disappears. That no longer smells like a miracle.
Nunca olió a milagro. Olió a lo de siemp: It never smelled like a miracle. It smelled like the usual: a strong one dealing damage to a helpless target.
Si quiere entender el barrio de verdad,: If you want to truly understand the neighbourhood and who can come and go without anyone noticing, talk to Mrs. Carmen. The one from the geranium balcony. She knows everything before it happens.
Un encapuchado con botas caras y un anil: A hooded figure in expensive boots and a seal ring. I jot it in bold. Thanks, Tomás. And lower the blinds tonight.
La puerta del campanario estaba abierta: The bell tower's door was open that night.
No hace falta llamar. Doña Carmen ya est: There's no need to call. Mrs. Carmen is already on the balcony, among dripping geraniums, wrapped in a black cloak. She watches you walk down the street as if she’s been waiting for a late‑arriving guest.
Has tardado más de lo que pensaba, detec: You took longer than I thought, detective. Emilio, Rosa, Tomás… all before the old Carmen. Same as always. The last one they think of and the first to know.
¿Y qué sabe la vieja Carmen?: And what does the old woman Carmen know?
Sube. La puerta está abierta. En esta ca: Climb. The door is open. In this house, doors are always open for those who seek the truth. I close them for liars.
El piso huele a membrillo y a tiempo det: The apartment smells of quince and of time standing still. Clocks everywhere, none set to the same hour. She sits in a rocking chair by the window, from which you can see, just across, the bell tower of San José.
El Barrio Viejo guarda secretos, hija. Y: The Old Quarter holds secrets, kid. And I guard them all, because from this balcony I’ve been watching the living and the dead come and go for fifty years.
Entonces guárdeme uno esta noche, Doña C: Keep me one tonight, Mrs. Carmen. The one from the church. The one from Marta.
Esa pobre criatura. ¿Sabes qué es lo que: That poor creature. You know what gives this neighborhood the most fear? That here no one ever truly disappears. There’s always someone who saw it and just keeps quiet.
Señala con un dedo torcido hacia la vent: He points with a twisted finger toward the window, toward the black tower silhouetted against the lightning.
Esa noche, entre campanada y campanada,: That night, between one chime and the next, I heard one that shouldn’t have beaten. The tiny bell, the one for the dead, rang out of sync. Alone. I know that bell: it only rings if someone steps on the bell tower floor.
Y nadie sube al campanario durante la mi: And nobody climbs the bell tower during Mass, detective. No one. Unless you want to sneak down where no one's looking.
Abierta de par en par. Lo vi con estos o: Opened in a sweep. I saw it with these eyes when the storm ceased: a black rectangle at the top of the tower, like a mouth. By morning it was shut again. Someone had closed it. Some hurried person with a key.
\"¿Quién tiene llave del campanario?\": Who has the bell tower key?
Oficialmente, el sacristán. Ese pobre no: Officially, the sacristan. That poor man wouldn’t break a plate. But the keys, daughter, are lent. They get copied. They’re stolen.
Pregunta en la comisaría, cuando tengas: Check at the police station when you've got the nerve. Then ask who else had a copy of that key. That's where the big stuff really starts. That's where people start coughing and change the subject.
\"¿Vio usted a alguien en la torre?\": Did you see anyone in the tower?
Una silueta. Un momento, contra el respl:
A silhouette.  
One moment, against the glare of a ray.  
He was carrying something.  
With someone, I mean.  
On the shoulder, like a sack.
Y no era el jorobado del sacristán. Este:
And it wasn't the sacristan’s hunchback.  
This one walked straight.  
Tall.  
With a long coat the wind lifted like raven wings.
(El encapuchado de Tomás. El mismo abrig: Tomás's hooded figure. Same coat. Same man.
\"¿Por qué me cuenta todo esto a mí?\": Why am I the one listening to all this?
Porque Marta me subía la compra cuando m: Because Marta would bring me groceries when my legs failed me, without asking for anything. Because she was good without bragging, that is the only kind of good that counts.
Y porque tú, hija, no eres de las que se: And because you, daughter, aren't the type to give up. It shows in your stride. For others, rain makes them shrink; for you, it straightens you. Find the one who did this.
Una cosa más, y ya me callo. No es la pr: One more thing, and I'll be quiet. This isn't the first. Years ago, another girl. Another storm. They also said it was a miracle. They also closed the case quickly. Ask yourself why it always rains when a woman disappears in this neighborhood.
El campanario. Ahí está la salida que na:
The bell tower  
There's the exit nobody saw  
I already have what I need to get into the church
Ve con Dios, o sin él, que para lo que v: Go with God—or without Him; for what you’re about to find it might be better to go alone.  Bundle up, the tower’s cold and holds more than one chill.
Un pañuelo con las iniciales M.S. junto: A handkerchief with the initials M.S. beside the tower staircase.
La puerta de San José pesa como una losa: The door of St. Joseph weighs like a slab. Inside, the ship is empty and dark, lit only by the trembling red of the votive lamps. It smells of cold incense and wet stone.
Four clues. A scream near the altar. No one at the front door. A hooded man with a seal ring. And an open bell tower at the wrong time.
Todo apunta hacia arriba. A esa torre. R: Everything points upward. To that tower. Rosa watched the gate; nobody lied. Marta didn't exit through any doorway that opens to the street.
Ante el altar, un reclinatorio sigue caí: Before the altar, a reclinatory still lies on its side. No one has straightened it. The front row retains a candle consumed to the metal, white, before Saint Joseph’s image.
Su vela. La encendió antes de morir... o: Her candle. She lit it before dying… or before whatever that was. She was sitting here. She screamed here.
Junto al muro lateral, medio oculta tras: Next to the side wall, half‑hidden behind a confessional, a low vestibule. Framed. On the other side begins a spiral staircase that vanishes upward into darkness.
La puerta del campanario. Doña Carmen te: The bell tower door. Mrs. Carmen was right. Let’s step in.
Los peldaños de piedra están gastados y: The stone steps are worn and damp. The ladder creaks, goes up, goes up. At mid‑height the lantern catches something in the step’s dust: two parallel ruts.
Marcas de arrastre. Talones. Alguien sub: Dragging marks. Heels. Someone climbed a body up here, step by step. A body that wasn't cooperating.
Arriba, el campanario se abre a la noche: Up above, the bell tower opens to the night. The wind slips through the arches, swaying the bells’ ropes, which wail low. The neon city beats in the distance, indifferent.
Desde aquí se domina todo el barrio. Y h: From here you can see the whole neighborhood. And there’s a service ladder that drops outside, behind, into the alley—where no one looks.
En el suelo, junto al hueco de la escala: On the floor, next to the hole in the exterior staircase, something white pops out against the grime. You kneel. A cloth handkerchief, embroidered with care. In a corner, two initials: M. S.
Marta Soler. La subieron mientras el ray: Marta Soler. They hauled her up while the lightning blinded everyone, slipped her down the back stairway, and took her away through the alley. Storm alibi and tower exit.
En el barandal metálico, enganchada, una: On the metal rail, dangling, a strand of dark cloth, expensive. And a fresh mark: something heavy brushed down just now.
Paño fino. El abrigo del encapuchado. No: Fine cloth. The hooded man's coat. I have no doubt: it's the same man Tomas saw grabbing her by the arm.
Mirar el pañuelo a la luz: Look at the handkerchief in the light
Bajo la linterna, junto a las iniciales,: Under the flashlight, next to the initials, a brownish dry stain. And a faint, sweetish, chemical odor. It's not blood: it's something else.
